Just now

--

The Question
Every season, the UEFA Champions League delivers moments of brilliance, heartbreak, and surprise. But beneath the drama lies a deeper question: can we predict which team will win using statistics alone?
Goals win matches, everyone knows that. But do they tell the whole story? Does possession matter? Is a strong defence more important than a prolific attack?
In this post, I break down the 2024–25 UCL squad data from FBref, build a weighted scoring formula, and test which combination of stats best predicts knockout-stage success.
The Data
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The analysis uses three datasets from FBref: squad attack stats (goals scored, possession, age), opponent stats (goals conceded per team from the opponent perspective), and a combined table for all 32 UCL group-stage teams.
After cleaning team names and converting columns to numeric types, the combined dataset gives us the core metrics: Goals Scored (GF), Goals Conceded (GA), and Possession for each team.
Key Findings
Press enter or click to view image in full size

1. Attack wins matches, defence wins tournaments. The balanced model outperformed both attack-heavy and defence-first variants, suggesting elite teams need strength on both ends.
2. Possession is overrated. The modest correlation with goals scored (r = 0.42) suggests efficient finishing and defensive shape matter more than simply keeping the ball.
3. Goal difference is a reliable shortcut. Even simple GD placed most actual top-8 teams in the upper tier.
